name: Eritrea
tagline: From the Red Sea ports of Aksum to Africa's longest war of independence and one of its most closed states.
summary: Eritrea occupies the southwestern shore of the Red Sea, a corridor where Africa, Arabia and the Mediterranean world have met for three thousand years. Its highlands and coast formed part of the heartland of the Kingdom of Aksum, whose port of Adulis carried ivory, gold and incense to Rome, India and Arabia, while the Dahlak Islands and Massawa became centres of Red Sea and Indian Ocean trade. After Aksum's decline the coast passed through Beja, Muslim and then Ottoman hands, and in the nineteenth century through Egyptian control, before Italy proclaimed Eritrea its 'first-born colony' in 1890 and built Asmara into a modernist colonial capital. Italy used Eritrea as the springboard for its invasions of Ethiopia, including the army shattered at Adwa in 1896 and the conquest of 1935-36. British forces ended Italian rule in 1941; in 1952 the United Nations federated Eritrea with Ethiopia, only for Emperor Haile Selassie to annex it outright in 1962. That act opened a thirty-year war of independence, waged first by the Eritrean Liberation Front and then by the Eritrean People's Liberation Front, which captured Asmara in 1991 as the Derg fell in Ethiopia. A referendum in 1993 brought formal independence under Isaias Afwerki, but a ruinous 1998-2000 border war with Ethiopia and decades of one-party rule have made Eritrea one of the most isolated countries on earth.

founding.label: Independence from Ethiopia
founding.detail: Eritrea's modern statehood was won by armed struggle and confirmed by ballot. After three decades of war, Eritrean People's Liberation Front fighters entered Asmara on 24 May 1991, ending Ethiopian rule de facto as the Marxist Derg collapsed in Addis Ababa. The EPLF formed a provisional government but deferred a final decision to the Eritrean people. In a UN-monitored referendum held over 23-25 April 1993, an overwhelming majority voted for independence, and on 24 May 1993 Eritrea formally proclaimed itself a sovereign state, joining the United Nations days later. Isaias Afwerki, the EPLF's wartime leader, became its first and, decades on, still only president.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: 24 May 1993 from Ethiopia; de facto May 1991 (EPLF victory)

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Asmara, a UNESCO-listed modernist colonial city (inscribed 2017)

quickFacts.label: War of independence
quickFacts.value: 1961-1991, roughly thirty years; among Africa's longest

quickFacts.label: Peoples
quickFacts.value: Nine recognised groups; Tigrinya and Tigre form about 80%, with Saho, Afar, Bilen, Kunama, Nara, Hedareb (Beja) and Rashaida

quickFacts.label: Working languages
quickFacts.value: Tigrinya, Arabic and English; no single official language is decreed

quickFacts.label: Government
quickFacts.value: One-party state under Isaias Afwerki; no national elections held since independence

eras.title: Aksum, Adulis and the Red Sea Trade
eras.period: c. 1st century CE - c. 7th century CE
eras.standfirst: The Eritrean coast and highlands lay at the heart of the Kingdom of Aksum, whose port of Adulis linked Africa to Rome, Arabia and India.
body: From around the first century CE the Kingdom of Aksum rose across the northern highlands of present-day Eritrea and Ethiopia to become one of the great trading powers of the ancient world. Its outlet to the sea was Adulis, on the Gulf of Zula near modern Massawa, named in the first-century Greek navigational manual the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea as a regulated port where Aksumite authorities controlled commerce. Through Adulis flowed ivory, gold, incense, tortoise shell and other African goods bound for Roman Egypt, South Arabia, Persia and India, and in return came cloth, metalware, wine and glass.

Adulis made Aksum a maritime as well as a highland power, and a contemporary writer ranked the kingdom alongside Rome, Persia and China. Aksumite kings minted their own gold, silver and bronze coinage and, in the fourth century, adopted Christianity, an identity that endured long after the kingdom's political eclipse. The coast and the Dahlak Archipelago offshore were stitched into a wider Red Sea and Indian Ocean world whose currents of trade, faith and language would shape the region for centuries.

From the late sixth and seventh centuries Aksum declined. Shifting trade routes, the exhaustion of resources and, above all, the rise of Islam along the opposite Arabian shore drew commerce away from Adulis, which fell into obscurity. Its ruins, near the Eritrean coast, were rediscovered and excavated by European and later Eritrean and British archaeologists, and remain a touchstone of Eritrea's claim to one of Africa's oldest civilisations.

events.title: Adulis named in the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea
events.summary: The Greek navigational handbook describes Adulis as the principal, legally regulated port serving the Aksumite hinterland, trading ivory, rhinoceros horn, tortoise shell and other goods.

events.title: Aksum emerges as a Red Sea trading empire
events.summary: Aksum consolidates control of the northern highlands and the port of Adulis, growing into a major commercial power linking Rome, Arabia and India.

events.title: Aksumite coinage
events.summary: Aksumite kings begin minting gold, silver and bronze coins, advertising the kingdom's wealth and its place among the great powers of the age.

events.title: Decline of Aksum and Adulis
events.summary: Shifting trade routes and the rise of Islam on the Arabian shore erode Red Sea commerce; Adulis fades and Aksum's power contracts inland.

pullquote.text: Adulis was the principal seaport of the Aksumite kingdom, a gateway through which Africa traded with Rome, Arabia and India.
pullquote.attribution: Summary of the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea and Adulis excavations

eras.title: The Beja, the Dahlak Sultanate and the Muslim Coast
eras.standfirst: After Aksum, the coast became a Muslim-dominated trading world of the Beja, the Dahlak Islands and the rising port of Massawa, set against the Christian highlands.
body: As Aksum waned, the Eritrean lowlands and coast passed into the orbit of the Cushitic-speaking Beja, whose semi-nomadic confederacies dominated the caravan routes and coastal trade from around the eighth century. The offshore Dahlak Archipelago became a centre of Red Sea commerce, and with the rise of the Sultanate of Dahlak from the eleventh century its rulers controlled the exchange between the Muslim coast and the Christian highlands, profiting from pearl fisheries and the trade in goods and enslaved people.

Massawa, sheltered by its islands, gradually overtook the older harbours and grew into the chief link between the interior and the wider Indian Ocean world. The peoples who make up modern Eritrea took shape across these centuries: the Tigrinya-speaking Christians of the highlands, the Tigre and Beja of the northern lowlands, and the Afar and Saho of the coast and southern deserts, alongside Bilen, Kunama, Nara and others. The highland Christian polity of Medri Bahri ('land of the sea') emerged to contest the coast with these lowland and Muslim powers.

Islam, carried across the narrow sea, became deeply rooted along the coast and in the lowlands even as the highlands remained a stronghold of Orthodox Christianity, a religious geography that still defines Eritrea. The era closed when a new imperial power reached the Red Sea: in 1557 Ottoman forces seized Massawa, ending the long age of local and dynastic control of the coast.

events.title: Beja ascendancy on the coast
events.summary: After the fall of Aksum, Beja confederacies dominate the caravan routes and coastal trade of what is now Eritrea, holding sway in the Massawa region for centuries.

events.title: Rise of the Sultanate of Dahlak
events.summary: The Dahlak Islands become a wealthy Muslim sultanate controlling pearl fisheries and the trade between the Red Sea and the Christian highlands.

events.title: Massawa grows as a leading port
events.summary: Massawa develops into the principal harbour of the coast, mediating trade between the highland interior and the Arabian Peninsula.

events.title: Ottomans capture Massawa
events.summary: Ottoman forces under Ozdemir Pasha take Massawa from the highland kingdom, bringing the Eritrean coast under the Ottoman Empire and ending local dynastic control.

eras.title: Ottomans, Egyptians and the Contested Coast
eras.standfirst: For three centuries the Red Sea coast was ruled at arm's length from Constantinople and Cairo, while the highlands stayed beyond imperial reach.
body: From 1557 the Ottomans held Massawa, the Dahlak Islands and stretches of the coast as part of their Habesh province, governing through local agents and exacting customs on the Red Sea trade. Ottoman power on this far frontier was always thin, and real control over the harbours often rested with hereditary local rulers, the naibs of the Massawa region, who balanced between the empire, the highland Christian kingdom and the lowland peoples of the interior.

In the nineteenth century, as Ottoman authority faded, Egypt under its ambitious Khedives extended its reach down the African Red Sea coast. Egyptian forces took over the administration of Massawa, holding it from the 1860s into the 1880s and pressing inland toward the highlands, where they clashed with the Ethiopian empire. Egyptian rule entrenched the coast's role as a customs and garrison zone but never absorbed the highland interior.

By the 1880s Egypt, bankrupt and convulsed by the Mahdist revolt in Sudan, withdrew from the coast. The vacuum it left on this strategically vital shore, opposite the newly opened Suez Canal route, drew in a new and far more permanent power. Italy, already established at the port of Assab, moved to occupy Massawa in 1885, setting the stage for the creation of a single colony out of the patchwork of coast and highland.

events.title: Ottoman Habesh province established
events.summary: After taking Massawa, the Ottomans organise their Red Sea holdings as the province of Habesh, ruling the coast through local agents.

events.title: Egyptian administration of Massawa
events.summary: Egypt, with Ottoman consent, takes over the administration of Massawa and the coast, extending its influence toward the highlands.

events.title: Italy gains a foothold at Assab
events.summary: An Italian shipping company acquires the bay of Assab on the southern Red Sea coast, Italy's first toehold on the future Eritrean shore.

events.title: Italy occupies Massawa
events.summary: As Egypt withdraws amid the Mahdist crisis, Italian troops occupy Massawa, the key port of the coast, beginning Italy's drive to build a colony.

eras.title: Italy's First-Born Colony
eras.standfirst: Italy proclaimed Eritrea its colonia primogenita in 1890, built Asmara into a modernist capital, and used the territory as the base for its wars on Ethiopia.
body: On 1 January 1890 Italy formally united its Red Sea possessions into a single colony, Eritrea, named from the Latin for the Red Sea (Mare Erythraeum). It was promoted as Italy's colonia primogenita, the 'first-born colony', and held a larger Italian settler population than Rome's later acquisitions in Somalia and Libya. The colonial capital, first at Massawa, was moved up to the cooler highland town of Asmara in 1897, which over the following decades was rebuilt as a planned modernist city of cinemas, garages and rationalist and Art Deco buildings, later inscribed by UNESCO as 'Asmara: A Modernist City of Africa'.

Eritrea served above all as the military springboard for Italian designs on Ethiopia. Pushing south from the colony, an Italian army was annihilated by Emperor Menelik II at the Battle of Adwa on 1 March 1896, the most crushing defeat of a European force in Africa, which fixed Eritrea's southern border and checked Italian ambitions for a generation. Italy invested in roads, the highland railway to Asmara and Massawa, plantations and industry, but ruled through land confiscation, forced labour and a rigid racial segregation that subordinated Eritreans while recruiting tens of thousands of them as colonial soldiers, the ascari.

Under Fascism the colony's military role returned. In October 1935 Mussolini launched the conquest of Ethiopia from Eritrea, and after Adwa and Aksum fell and Addis Ababa was taken in May 1936, Eritrea was folded into Italian East Africa. The empire was short-lived. In the Second World War, British, Commonwealth and Free French forces broke Italian resistance at the long Battle of Keren in early 1941; Asmara and Massawa surrendered, and half a century of Italian rule ended.

events.title: Colony of Eritrea proclaimed
events.summary: Italy unites its Red Sea holdings into the colony of Eritrea, promoted as its colonia primogenita or 'first-born colony', with the capital initially at Massawa.

events.title: Battle of Adwa
events.summary: Advancing from Eritrea, an Italian army is destroyed by Emperor Menelik II at Adwa, the worst defeat of a European army in Africa, securing Ethiopian sovereignty and fixing Eritrea's southern frontier.

events.title: Asmara becomes the colonial capital
events.summary: The colonial seat is moved from Massawa to the highland town of Asmara, later rebuilt as a planned modernist city.

events.title: Conquest of Ethiopia launched from Eritrea
events.summary: Italian forces invade Ethiopia from Eritrea, taking Addis Ababa in May 1936; Eritrea is absorbed into Italian East Africa.

events.title: British defeat Italy at Keren
events.summary: Allied forces overcome Italian troops at the Battle of Keren; Asmara and Massawa surrender, ending Italian rule and beginning British military administration.

figures.name: Hamid Idris Awate
figures.role: Eritrean nationalist; fired the first shots of the independence war
figures.blurb: A lowland fighter who, on 1 September 1961, opened fire on Ethiopian forces at Mount Adal, an act remembered as the beginning of the Eritrean armed struggle.

pullquote.text: Asmara is an exceptionally well-preserved example of a colonial planned city, resulting from successive phases of planning between 1893 and 1941 under Italian occupation.

eras.title: Federation, Annexation and the Thirty-Year War
eras.standfirst: British administration gave way to a UN federation with Ethiopia in 1952; its abolition in 1962 ignited a thirty-year war of independence.
body: British military administration governed Eritrea from 1941 while the Allies and then the new United Nations debated its fate. In 1950 the UN General Assembly resolved, as an American-backed compromise between full independence and union with Ethiopia, that Eritrea should become an autonomous unit federated with Ethiopia under the Ethiopian crown. The federation took effect on 11 September 1952, leaving Eritrea its own parliament, flag and control of domestic affairs within an Ethiopian-led union.

Emperor Haile Selassie steadily hollowed out that autonomy, banning Eritrean parties and newspapers, imposing Amharic and replacing Eritrean symbols. In November 1962 his government had the Eritrean assembly dissolve the federation, annexing Eritrea as an Ethiopian province. By then armed resistance had already begun: on 1 September 1961 Hamid Idris Awate and a small band of the Eritrean Liberation Front fired on Ethiopian forces, opening a war that would last three decades.

Through the 1970s leadership of the struggle passed from the ELF to the more disciplined, Marxist Eritrean People's Liberation Front. The conflict ground on through Haile Selassie's fall, the rise of Ethiopia's Marxist Derg under Mengistu, massive Soviet-backed offensives and recurrent famine. The EPLF dug into the northern mountains, ran its own clandestine administration and, from the late 1980s, went onto the offensive. In May 1991, as the Derg collapsed, EPLF fighters entered Asmara on 24 May, winning de facto independence after one of Africa's longest wars.

events.title: British military administration begins
events.summary: Following the Allied victory at Keren, Britain administers Eritrea for the next decade while its post-war status is debated.

events.title: UN federation with Ethiopia
events.summary: Under UN Resolution 390 A (V) of 1950, Eritrea becomes an autonomous unit federated with Ethiopia, keeping its own parliament, flag and control of internal affairs.

events.title: Armed struggle begins at Mount Adal
events.summary: Hamid Idris Awate and a small ELF band fire on Ethiopian forces, the conventional start of the thirty-year Eritrean War of Independence.

events.title: Haile Selassie annexes Eritrea
events.summary: After steadily eroding Eritrean autonomy, the emperor dissolves the federation and annexes Eritrea as a province, hardening the independence war.

events.title: Rise of the EPLF
events.summary: The Eritrean People's Liberation Front splits from the ELF and becomes the dominant, Marxist-led nationalist movement waging the war.

events.title: EPLF captures Asmara
events.summary: As the Derg regime collapses in Ethiopia, EPLF forces enter Asmara, winning de facto independence and forming a provisional government.

figures.name: Haile Selassie
figures.blurb: The Ethiopian emperor whose dismantling of Eritrean autonomy and 1962 annexation of the territory turned a federation into a province and helped ignite the long war of independence.

pullquote.text: Eritrea was to constitute an autonomous unit federated with Ethiopia under the sovereignty of the Ethiopian Crown.

eras.title: Independence, Border War and the Closed State
eras.standfirst: A 1993 referendum brought formal independence; a devastating border war with Ethiopia and decades of one-party rule have left Eritrea among the world's most closed states.
body: The EPLF translated victory into statehood through the ballot. In a UN-monitored referendum held on 23-25 April 1993, Eritreans voted overwhelmingly for independence, reported at nearly unanimous approval on a high turnout. On 24 May 1993 Eritrea formally declared itself sovereign and, within days, joined the United Nations as its 182nd member. Isaias Afwerki, the EPLF's wartime leader, became president, and the movement reconstituted itself as the ruling People's Front for Democracy and Justice.

Hopes of a democratic dividend faded. A ratified constitution was never implemented, promised elections were never held, and dissent was crushed. The fragile peace with Ethiopia broke down over the contested border town of Badme, igniting a full-scale war from May 1998 to June 2000 that killed an estimated tens of thousands on each side and displaced more than a million people. The Algiers Agreement of December 2000 ended the fighting and a boundary commission awarded Badme to Eritrea, but Ethiopia refused to withdraw, leaving a tense 'no war, no peace' stand-off.

Citing that unresolved confrontation, the government entrenched one of the world's most repressive systems: open-ended national service, severe restrictions on movement, press and religion, and no independent courts or legislature, conditions documented by United Nations investigators and Human Rights Watch and driving large numbers of Eritreans into exile. A surprise rapprochement came in July 2018, when Isaias Afwerki and Ethiopia's new prime minister Abiy Ahmed signed a peace declaration formally ending the border war. Yet little changed internally, and Eritrea has remained a tightly closed, single-party state.

events.title: Independence referendum
events.summary: Eritreans vote overwhelmingly for independence in a UN-monitored referendum, with near-unanimous approval on a high turnout.

events.title: Formal independence
events.summary: Eritrea proclaims itself a sovereign state and is admitted to the United Nations days later; Isaias Afwerki becomes its first president.

events.title: Border war with Ethiopia
events.summary: A dispute over Badme escalates into a full-scale war that kills tens of thousands and displaces more than a million before a ceasefire.

events.title: Algiers Agreement
events.summary: Eritrea and Ethiopia sign the Algiers peace agreement; a boundary commission later awards Badme to Eritrea, but Ethiopia refuses to withdraw, leaving a frozen stand-off.

events.title: Entrenched one-party rule
events.summary: Promised elections and the ratified constitution are shelved; indefinite national service and bans on independent press, parties and religion make Eritrea one of the world's most closed states.

events.title: Peace with Ethiopia
events.summary: Isaias Afwerki and Abiy Ahmed sign a declaration formally ending the border war, though internal repression in Eritrea continues largely unchanged.

figures.name: Isaias Afwerki
figures.role: EPLF leader and first president of Eritrea
figures.blurb: Wartime commander of the EPLF who became Eritrea's first president on independence in 1993 and has ruled the one-party state ever since, without national elections.

pullquote.text: Eritrea has no constitution in force, no national legislature, no independent press and no elections; national service for many is open-ended.
pullquote.attribution: Paraphrase of Human Rights Watch reporting on Eritrea

figures.name: Isaias Afwerki
figures.role: EPLF leader; first and only president of Eritrea
figures.blurb: Led the Eritrean People's Liberation Front to victory in 1991 and became president on independence in 1993; he has since governed Eritrea as a tightly controlled one-party state with no national elections.

figures.name: Hamid Idris Awate
figures.blurb: Credited with firing the first shots of the Eritrean War of Independence at Mount Adal on 1 September 1961, a date commemorated in Eritrea as the start of the revolution.

figures.name: Haile Selassie
figures.blurb: His 1962 annexation of Eritrea, after years of eroding its UN-granted autonomy, transformed a federation into outright rule and helped trigger the thirty-year independence war.

sources.label: The Ancient Red Sea Port of Adulis, Eritrea (Eritro-British Expedition report)
Asmara: A Modernist City of Africa
A Guide to the United States' History of Recognition: Eritrea
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

sources.label: Eritrea: The Independence Struggle and the Struggles of Independence
sources.publisher: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS)

sources.label: Eritrea-Ethiopia Peace Deal Offers Hope for Reform
